title: Notes on Deep Learning for Coders
text:
Before you begin setting up a deep learning network, make sure you split your dataset into three subsets: training, validation, and testing. The training set is used to fit the parameters (this is what we mean by training the model), the validation set is used to adjust the hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, number of layers, etc.) and to evaluate how well the model is generalizing (and not overfitting), and the testing set is used as a final measure of how well the model performs on an unseen data. This figure shows how the dataset should be splitted:




flowchart TB
    dataset[(Dataset)] --> train[(Training set)]
    dataset --> valid[(Validation set)]
    dataset --> test[(Testing set)]





 The validation set is data that is hidden from the model, and the testing set is data that is hidden from human modelers (people who are adjusting the hyperparameters). We use the validation set to prevent the model from memorizing the training set instead of generalizing, and we use the testing set to prevent the human modelers from adjusting too much of the hyperparameters to fit the validation set rather than on unseen data that the model is supposed to perform on.
Note that depending on data availability, having a testing set may not always be possible. However, it is crucial to at least have a validation set.



These figures are my attempt to summarize a typical single epoch of training a deep learning model. Remember that training is a machine learning term for finding the most optimal parameters. Some processes may vary depending on the specific model and techniques used.
Figure 1 (Training set):




flowchart TB
    train[(Training set)]
    indep(Independent variable)
    depen(Dependent variable)
    arch{Architecture}
    param(Current parameters)
    newpa(Updated parameters)
    pred[(Prediction)]
    lossfn[[Loss function]]
    metric[[Metric function]]
    met(Batch accuracy)
    loss(Training loss)
    gdfn[[Backpropagation]]
    lr(Learning rate)
    gd(Gradient of parameters)
    step[[Gradient descent]]

    train --> indep
    train --> depen
    indep --> arch
    depen --> lossfn
    depen --> metric
    param --> arch
    arch --> pred
    pred --> lossfn
    pred --> metric
    metric --> met
    lossfn --> loss
    lossfn --> gdfn
    param --> gdfn
    gdfn --> gd
    gd --> step
    lr --> step
    step --> newpa





 Figure 2 (Validation set):




flowchart TB
    valid[(Validation set)]
    param(Current parameters)
    indep(Independent variable)
    depen(Dependent variable)
    arch{Architecture}
    pred[(Prediction)]
    lossfn[[Loss function]]
    loss(Validation loss)

    valid --> indep
    valid --> depen
    indep --> arch
    param --> arch
    depen --> lossfn
    arch --> pred
    pred --> lossfn
    lossfn --> loss





 An architecture is composed of an input layer, hidden layers, and an output layer. The function of the input layer is to receive the input data (independent variable) and pass it to the hidden layers. The function of the hidden layers is then to pick up on the patterns by extracting and combining different features1 of the input data (technically, of the previous layer) in an increasing level of generalization.
More specifically, each hidden layer is made up of neurons. These neurons produce outputs, known as activations. The activations are calculated by taking the dot product of the weights2 and the activations from the previous layer, adding a bias, and then passing the result through an activation function.

    lr(Learning rate)

    pred --> metric
    metric --> met
    lossfn --> loss
    lossfn --> gdfn
    param --> gdfn
    gdfn --> gd
    gd --> step
    lr --> step
    step --> newpa





 Figure 2 (Validation set):




flowchart TB
    valid[(Validation set)]
    param(Current parameters)
    indep(Independent variable)
    depen(Dependent variable)
    arch{Architecture}
    pred[(Prediction)]

    loss(Validation loss)

    valid --> indep
    valid --> depen
    indep --> arch
    param --> arch
    depen --> lossfn
    arch --> pred
    pred --> lossfn
    lossfn --> loss





 An architecture is composed of an input layer, hidden layers, and an output layer. The function of the input layer is to receive the input data (independent variable) and pass it to the hidden layers. The function of the hidden layers is then to pick up on the patterns by extracting and combining different features1 of the input data (technically, of the previous layer) in an increasing level of generalization.
More specifically, each hidden layer is made up of neurons. These neurons produce outputs, known as activations. The activations are calculated by taking the dot product of the weights2 and the activations from the previous layer, adding a bias, and then passing the result through an activation function.

title: Notes on Deep Learning for Coders
text:
Footnotes


Features are measurable characteristics or properties of the input data. Features can be as simple as individual pixel values of an image or as complex as an existence of a particular shape in the input data.↩︎
Weights and biases are what is known as parameters of a neural network.↩︎

title: Jiho Kim
text:
Hi there! I am a junior at Calvin University studying Computer Science. I am currently working as a research assistant with Dr. Kenneth Arnold. We are researching an AI writing tool that gives users more creative freedom and control over their content.
My research interests are in human-computer interaction, natural language processing, and data science. I am actively seeking research opportunities and hoping to pursue a PhD in the future.
